query: Glycogen synthesis and breakdown in liver

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Glycogenolysis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogen, also known as animal starch, is a branched polysaccharide that serves as an energy reserve in the liver and muscle. It is readily available as an immediate source of energy. The formation of glycogen from glucose is called glycogenesis, and the breakdown of glycogen to form glucose is called glycogen metabolism or glycogenolysis. Increased cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) produces the breakdown of glycogen (glycogenolysis). Various hormones such as insulin, glucagon, cortisol, and others regulate the relationship between glycogenesis and gluconeogenesis. Glycogenolysis is initiated by the action of a specific enzyme called phosphorylase, which yields glucose-1-phosphate. Glucose-1-phosphate is an important compound at the intersection of several metabolic pathways, such as glycolysis, glycogenesis, glycogenolysis, and gluconeogenesis. Interestingly, when glycogenesis occurs, glycogenolysis is inhibited, and vice versa. Impaired glycogen metabolism is associated with various inherited metabolic disorders collectively known as glycogen storage diseases. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry - Glycogenolysis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogen, also known as animal starch, is a branched polysaccharide that serves as a reserve of carbohydrates in the body; it is stored in the liver and muscle and readily available as an immediate energy source. The formation of glycogen from glucose is known as glycogenesis, and the breakdown of glycogen to form glucose is called glycogen metabolism or glycogenolysis. Increased cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) catalyzes the breakdown of glycogen (glycogenolysis). The primary hormones that regulate the cycle of glycogenesis and gluconeogenesis are insulin, glucagon, and cortisol. Glycogenolysis is initiated by the action of a specific enzyme called phosphorylase, which yields glucose-1-phosphate (P). Glucose-1-P is an essential compound at the intersection of several metabolic pathways, such as glycolysis, glycogenesis, glycogenolysis, and gluconeogenesis. When glycogenesis occurs, glycogenolysis is inhibited, and vice versa. Impaired glycogen metabolism, characterized by abnormal glycogen accumulation, is associated with inherited metabolic disorders and is collectively known as glycogen storage diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Glycogenolysis -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The liver breaks down glycogen to maintain adequate blood glucose levels, whereas muscles break down glycogen to maintain energy for contraction.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Glycogenolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The key regulatory enzymes of glycogenolysis are phosphorylase kinase and glycogen phosphorylase, both activated by phosphorylation. These are predominantly expressed in the liver, muscle, and brain. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry - Glycogenolysis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogenolysis (glycogen metabolism) is initiated by the action of an enzyme known as phosphorylase. Phosphorylase catalyzes the phosphorolytic cleavage of α 1→ 4 linkage to produce glucose-1-P. The liver and muscle both contain the enzyme phosphorylase. Phosphorylase activity is regulated by phosphorylation; in the phosphorylated form, it is active, and in the non-phosphorylated form, it is inactive. The liver breaks down glycogen during periods of fasting to increase the blood glucose concentration for use as fuel by the body, particularly in the brain and red blood cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Glycogen -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogen is an extensively branched glucose polymer that animals use as an energy reserve. It is the animal analog to starch. Glycogen does not exist in plant tissue. It is highly concentrated in the liver, although skeletal muscles contain the most glycogen by weight. It is also present in lower levels in other tissues, such as the kidney, heart, and brain. [1] [2] The glucose residues within glycogen connect via two principal bonds, the alpha-1,4, and alpha-1,6 glycosidic bonds in linear strands and at junction points.  The branching is a crucial aspect of glycogen as it increases its solubility and allows it to get metabolized more quickly. [3] Importantly, glycogen serves to maintain glucose homeostasis in the animal body.  Because of this, its metabolism is regulated primarily by insulin and glucagon and molecules in their downstream signaling pathways. Insulin and glucagon promote glycogen synthesis and breakdown, respectively. Pathologies that target enzymes involved in glycogen synthesis, degradation, and/or regulation may have significant adverse effects on the body. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Metabolism -- Mechanism -- Carbohydrate Metabolism
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogen is a polymer of glucose, assembled by the glycogen synthase, with branch points every 10 glucose molecules, which gives the glycogen a tree-like structure, which is beneficial for glucose mobilization. Some tissues utilize glycogen for their maintenance, like skeletal muscle; some other tissues use glycogen to maintain serum glucose levels stable, such as the liver. The liver can store almost 100 g of glycogen that supplies glucose for 24 hours; the skeletal muscle stores 350 g, which is sufficient for 60 minutes of muscle contraction. Glucose is metabolized by glycolysis in all cells to form pyruvate. This process does not use oxygen, and it generates 2 molecules of pyruvate, 2 NADH and 2 ATP.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Glycogenolysis -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenal hormones, such as catecholamines and glucocorticoids, regulate hepatic glycogenolysis. Adenosine triggers the release of corticosterone from the adrenal glands, which in turn stimulates the breakdown of glycogen in the liver. [1] By responding to norepinephrine via a cAMP-dependent mechanism, glycogenolysis maintains stability during hypoglycemia. Glycogenolysis generates energy from ATP, NADH, and lactate production. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glucose -- Organ Systems Involved -- Liver
retrieved_docs.content: The liver plays a crucial role in regulating blood glucose levels. Glycogen, a multibranched polysaccharide of glucose, is the storage form of glucose in the human body, primarily found in the liver and skeletal muscle. Glycogen functions as the body's short-term storage of glucose, whereas triglycerides in adipose tissues serve as the long-term storage. Glucose is released from glycogen when stimulated by glucagon and during fasting conditions, thereby increasing blood glucose levels. Glucose is added to glycogen under the control of insulin and energy-rich conditions, lowering blood glucose levels. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry - Glycogenolysis -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogen levels are quantitively more present in skeletal muscle than in the liver. Nevertheless, glycogenolysis serves important roles in both tissues. In the liver, glycogen metabolism is vital during fasting conditions, leading to hepatic glucose production to maintain healthy blood glucose levels and support the body's energy needs. On the other hand, glycogen in skeletal muscle indicates a critical function in terms of rapid ATP generation. A close relationship exists between glycogen storage in skeletal muscle and fatigue resistance. The ability of a muscle to exercise during the first 30 minutes of activity, despite the abundance of other energy sources such as lipids, is severely compromised when glycogen levels are diminished in skeletal muscle. Glycogen depletion is shown to cause fatigue because the muscle cannot provide adequate fuel to skeletal muscle for excitation and contraction. A possible reason is related to the role of glycogen in releasing calcium from the sarcoplasmic reticulum. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry - Glycogenolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Nevertheless, in gluconeogenic organs such as the liver, kidney, and intestines, glucose-6-phosphate is dephosphorylated to glucose—with the aid of the enzyme glucose-6-phosphatase—so that it can undergo transport from the endoplasmic reticulum to the interstitial space. Corresponding to the lysosomal glycogen degradation, the primary enzyme involved in acid maltase. The hydrolysis of glycogen to glucose, catalyzed by the acid alpha-glucosidase, is hypothesized to serve as a protective mechanism for the liver from high glycogen concentrations. Of the total glycogenolysis in skeletal muscle, only 5% of glycogen degradation happens in lysosomes. For liver glycogenolysis, only 10% occurs in lysosomes. [6] [5] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Insulin Metabolic Effects -- Function -- Role in Glycogen Metabolism
retrieved_docs.content: In the liver, insulin affects glycogen metabolism by stimulating glycogen synthesis. Protein phosphatase I (PPI) is the key molecule in regulating glycogen metabolism. Via dephosphorylation, PPI slows the rate of glycogenolysis by inactivating phosphorylase kinase and phosphorylase A. In contrast, PPI accelerates glycogenesis by activating glycogen synthase B. Insulin increases PPI substrate-specific activity on glycogen particles, stimulating glycogen synthesis from glucose in the liver.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry - Glycogenolysis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Muscles use glycogen to generate energy, particularly during anaerobic glycolysis in a "flight or fight" situation like intense exercise. Adrenaline triggers the breakdown of glycogen by binding to its receptor and activating adenylate cyclase, which produces cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP). Increased cAMP activates a cascade of biochemical events that leads to stimulation of phosphorylase activity, which causes the breakdown of glycogen. Shortly after a meal high in carbohydrates, the liver may contain as much as 4% to 6% of glycogen. However, after approximately 12 to 18 hours of fasting, the liver is almost totally depleted of glycogen. Glycogenolysis, along with glycolysis, plays a central role in carbohydrate metabolism. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoglycemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: During fasting, glucose levels are maintained via gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis in the liver. Gluconeogenesis is the pathway in which glucose is generated from non-carbohydrate sources, such as protein, lipids, pyruvate, or lactate. In contrast, glycogenolysis is the breakdown of glycogen stored into glucose products. Much glycogenolysis occurs in hepatocytes (liver) and myocytes (muscle).

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogenic Hepatopathy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of glycogenic hepatopathy is incompletely understood. It is believed to be the consequence of recurrent fluctuations in glucose level with hyperglycemia, hypoglycemia, and hyper insulinization. Glycogenic hepatopathy occurs when prolonged or marked hyperglycemia is treated with supraphysiological amounts of insulin. [10] It has been reported first with hyperglycemia and hypoglycemia with ketoacidosis in type 1 DM. It has also been reported without ketosis or acidosis in patients with type 2 DM and with variable insulin requirements. [10] [18] Glycogen is the polymer of glucose. After feeding, the liver takes up glucose and utilizes it for fuel with the excess to be converted into glycogen. Glycogen is the reservoir for glucose. Glycogenosis and glycogenolysis keep the balance of glycogen levels in the liver. High glucose levels move into the hepatocytes via facilitated diffusion through GLUT 2 transporter, independent of insulin. [19] The glucose is trapped in the cell after irreversible phosphorylation to glucose-6 phosphate by the enzyme glucokinase. Further, hyperglycemia treated with high doses of insulin polymerizes the glucose 6-phosphate to glycogen by the enzyme glycogen synthase. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Metabolism -- Mechanism -- Carbohydrate Metabolism
retrieved_docs.content: Pyruvate may have 3 fates within the cell: it can be transported into the mitochondria and generate acetyl–CoA, it can remain in the cytosol and generate lactate, or it can be used in glyconeogenesis by the enzyme alanine aminotransferase (ALT). The fate of pyruvate in tissues depends on hormonal regulation, oxygen availability, and the particular tissue. For example, in the liver, excess pyruvate is metabolized to acetyl–CoA, and then this is used for lipid synthesis, whereas in muscle, it undergoes complete oxidation to CO2.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogenolytic hormones are a class of hormones that stimulate the breakdown of glycogen into glucose, thereby increasing blood glucose levels. Pancreatic hormones, which are secreted by the pancreas, fall within this category because they include glucagon, a key glycogenolytic hormone that promotes glycogenolysis. This relationship highlights the role of pancreatic endocrine function in regulating glucose metabolism through glycogenolytic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2773
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropetin and glukagon are both members of the broad class “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein.” As peptide hormones, they share the structural characteristic of being protein‑based signaling molecules, yet they serve very different physiological functions. Erythropetin regulates erythropoiesis by stimulating the production of red blood cells, while glukagon raises blood glucose levels by promoting glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Recognizing that these distinct hormones belong to the same molecular category highlights their common biochemical nature and informs both basic understanding of hormone biology and the development of protein‑based therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1951
retrieved_docs.content: BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS are classified as liver therapy agents with lipotropic properties, meaning they are used to support hepatic function and promote the metabolism and transport of fats within the liver.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1400
retrieved_docs.content: BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS represent a class of medications that function as bile therapy drugs and are simultaneously categorized under liver therapy and lipotropics. By acting as bile therapy agents, these drugs help promote bile production and flow, supporting digestive processes and hepatic clearance. Their classification as liver therapy agents reflects their broader role in enhancing overall liver function, protecting hepatocytes, and aiding recovery from liver injury. As lipotropic agents, they also facilitate the metabolism and mobilization of lipids, contributing to the regulation of fat processing in the body. The dual categorization underscores the overlapping therapeutic objectives of improving bile dynamics, supporting liver health, and optimizing lipid metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1977
retrieved_docs.content: Klofibrát produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, thereby influencing lipid metabolism, but it is contraindicated in patients with تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic steatosis). Consequently, clinicians should consider the lipid‑lowering benefit of Klofibrát while avoiding its use in individuals with hepatic steatosis to prevent potential liver‑related complications.

Finally, trisaccharides, as short carbohydrate chains, can serve as substrates or regulatory sugars that modulate glycosylation and other carbohydrate‑based biosynthetic processes. Together, these entities illustrate the wide range of chemical, pharmacologic, and biological factors that can define, regulate, or modify biosynthetic mechanisms, underscoring their importance for understanding metabolic regulation and for developing therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: The endokrinní systém (endocrine system) is the physiological network that regulates numerous metabolic processes and includes conditions such as متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) as a manifestation of its dysregulation. Within this system, pankreatické hormony (pancreatic hormones) represent a specific group of secretions that contribute to glucose homeostasis and other endocrine functions. Medical interventions that affect hormonal balance, such as kastrace (castration), are procedures that target the endokrinní systém, reflecting its role as a therapeutic focus in certain clinical contexts. Glycogenolytic hormones, which promote the breakdown of glycogen to glucose, constitute a distinct class of hormones that are not classified as pankreatické hormony, underscoring the diversity of hormonal actions within and beyond the endocrine system.

Overall, the network highlights the interrelationships among abdominal procedures, biliary imaging, liver pathology, detoxification therapies, and their diagnostic modalities in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2404
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) is a broad category of disease that includes carbohydrate metabolism disorder (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات) as one of its subtypes. Within the hierarchy of carbohydrate metabolism disorder, other endocrine disorders are classified as a further subset, indicating that these endocrine conditions are considered specific forms of carbohydrate metabolism disorders. This hierarchical relationship highlights how metabolic disorders encompass a range of more specialized conditions, including those affecting carbohydrate processing and related endocrine functions.

Enzymes for Alimentary Tract and Metabolism, a subclass of OTHER ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM PRODUCTS, contains recombinant enzyme replacement therapies imiglucerase (for Gaucher disease) and laronidase (for mucopolysaccharidosis I). Together, this structured taxonomy links chemical families, mechanisms of action (pro‑kinetic, serotonergic blockade, anticholinergic, opioid antagonism, laxative, bile‑acid supplementation, enzyme replacement) with their therapeutic indications, facilitating precise prescribing and research in gastroenterology, hepatobiliary disorders, and metabolic enzyme deficiencies.

Metabolic disorders of lipoproteins and other lipidemias are a subcategory of disorders of lipid metabolism, inversely linked (ISA) to the Arabic term اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي (metabolic disorder) and classified as اضطراب استقلاب الدهون (disorder of fat metabolism). This group maps to type IIa hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ) and includes early‑onset diabetes/hyperglycemia (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد), demonstrating clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and glucose‑regulation disorders. Autosomal dominant inheritance is the pattern for type IIa hyperlipoproteinemia, and the lipid‑modifying drug class fibrates (e.g., bezafibrát, clofibrate, fenofibrate) is used to treat these disorders; clofibrate is contraindicated in liver cirrhosis.
